_meta.purpose: Per-circuit tyre stress/abrasiveness characteristics, for sim's degradation-curve multipliers (currently flat placeholders per sim's SIMLOG.md item 9 follow-up). Consumed alongside tracks.json and track-confidence-lookup.json.

confidenceLegend.confirmed: Directly stated by Pirelli/FIA/F1 as an official rating for a specific season.
confidenceLegend.reasonable_estimate: Derived from Pirelli's own general methodology (traction/braking/lateral/track-evolution/asphalt-abrasion factors, as described in Pirelli's public 'how tyres are chosen' explainer) applied to well-known circuit characteristics, not a single official numeric table — no such table is publicly published in full, confirmed circuit-by-circuit, for a given season.

_meta.methodology: Pirelli rates circuits informally each race weekend on traction demands, braking demands, lateral-force demands, and overall tyre stress/asphalt abrasion, then picks 3 of 5 compounds accordingly (harder compounds for high-stress circuits, softer for low-stress). No single official numeric 1-5 table across all circuits for one season is publicly published in a form we could cite directly, so the abrasivenessRating (1=gentle on tyres, 5=very hard on tyres) below is a reasonable_estimate synthesis of widely-reported circuit reputations (race-weekend Pirelli preview notes, post-season technical review commentary), not a lifted official table. Treat as a starting multiplier basis for sim, not a calibrated value.

albert_park.basis: Smooth, newer asphalt (resurfaced for 2021 layout); low-medium tyre stress, mostly traction/braking-limited rather than lateral-load-limited.

shanghai.basis: Long, high-energy Turn 1-2-3 complex and a long back straight into heavy braking; moderate-high combined stress.

suzuka.basis: High-speed, high-lateral-load circuit (Esses, 130R, Spoon); consistently reported as one of the more physically demanding circuits on tyres.

bahrain.basis: Abrasive desert asphalt surface is a widely-reported characteristic of this circuit specifically (distinct from most other stress factors, this one is about surface grain rather than load), commonly cited as one of the most tyre-punishing surfaces on the calendar.

jeddah.basis: Very high average speed and sustained lateral loads through fast sweeping sections, offset by a smooth new-tarmac surface.

miami.basis: Mix of high-speed sections and tight chicanes; moderate stress, newer asphalt limits abrasion.

imola.basis: Old-school high-speed direction changes (Tamburello, Villeneuve) with some abrasive, bumpy surface sections.

monaco.basis: Low speeds throughout, minimal lateral load; consistently the lowest-degradation circuit on the calendar (strategy is track-position-dominated, not tyre-life-dominated, here).

barcelona.basis: High-speed, long-duration lateral loading (Turns 3, 9) on an abrasive surface; historically used as a pre-season tyre-development benchmark specifically because it's demanding.

montreal.basis: Stop-start, traction/braking-dominated rather than lateral-load-dominated; smooth surface, low-medium tyre stress despite being physically demanding on brakes.

red_bull_ring.basis: Short lap, mostly traction-zone-dominated (uphill/downhill throttle sections); low-medium tyre stress.

silverstone.basis: Sustained very-high-speed lateral loading (Maggotts-Becketts-Chapel, Copse) is consistently reported as among the single most tyre-punishing sequences in the sport; commonly cited as the most demanding circuit on the calendar for tyres.

spa.basis: Long circuit with some high-load corners (Eau Rouge/Raidillon, Pouhon) balanced by long low-load straight sections; moderate overall stress.

hungaroring.basis: Continuous medium-speed cornering with little rest for tyres, despite low top speeds; often described as deceptively demanding for a 'low-speed' track.

zandvoort.basis: Banked corners (Turn 3, final corner) increase lateral loading beyond what the circuit's short length would suggest.

monza.basis: Lowest-downforce, mostly-straight-line circuit on the calendar with heavy but brief braking zones; consistently one of the lowest-degradation circuits.

baku.basis: Long straight-line running with a tight, low-speed castle section; low-medium overall stress despite being a demanding circuit for other reasons (walls, braking into Turn 1).

singapore.basis: Long race duration (longest on the calendar by time), heavy braking zones, bumpy street surface, and heat/humidity combine for high cumulative tyre stress despite modest peak lateral loads.

cota.basis: Turn 1 uphill esses and a mix of high-speed direction changes create sustained lateral loading; widely reported as a demanding circuit for tyres.

mexico_city.basis: High altitude (thinner air) reduces aerodynamic loads and cornering forces on tyres despite the circuit's technical stadium section; commonly reported as a lower-degradation circuit for this reason specifically.

interlagos.basis: Anticlockwise direction (unusual loading pattern), undulating surface, and Senna S/Turn 4-5 loading create moderate-high stress.

las_vegas.basis: Very long straights, cold night-race track temperatures typically reduce degradation; only 2-3 real races run, low confidence in the exact rating.

lusail.basis: Very high-speed, high-lateral-load layout with minimal rest for tyres across a full lap; widely reported (including explicit compound-conservatism from Pirelli in past editions due to structural tyre stress concerns) as one of the most demanding circuits on the calendar.

yas_marina.basis: Modern, smooth surface with a mix of low-speed and medium-speed sections; generally reported as low-medium stress.

madring.basis: New venue, no real-world race data yet (first race Sept 2026).
